questions.stem: Homeostasis is the maintenance of:

options.text: A stable internal environment within limits

questions.explanation: Homeostasis maintains the internal environment within preset limits despite external fluctuations.

questions.stem: Which is a homeostatic variable regulated in humans?
questions.explanation: Homeostatic variables in humans include body temperature, blood pH, blood glucose and blood osmotic concentration.

questions.stem: Homeostasis mainly uses which type of feedback control?
questions.explanation: Homeostasis uses negative feedback, which returns variables to the set point from both above and below.

questions.scenario: A homeostatic variable rises above its set point.
questions.stem: Negative feedback will:

options.text: Return the variable back toward the set point
Push it further above the set point

questions.explanation: Negative feedback returns a variable to the set point whether it is above or below the set value.

questions.scenario: Blood glucose rises after a meal.
questions.stem: Which hormone is secreted to lower it?
questions.explanation: Insulin, secreted by pancreatic cells, lowers blood glucose; glucagon raises it.

questions.scenario: Blood glucose falls too low.
questions.stem: Which hormone is secreted to raise it?
questions.explanation: Glucagon raises blood glucose; insulin lowers it. Both are secreted by pancreatic endocrine cells.

questions.stem: Insulin and glucagon are secreted by which organ?
questions.explanation: Pancreatic endocrine cells secrete insulin and glucagon to regulate blood glucose.

questions.scenario: A person's cells do not respond well to insulin.
questions.stem: This physiological change is associated with:
questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes involves reduced response to insulin; type 1 involves insufficient insulin production.

questions.stem: Type 1 diabetes is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Type 1 diabetes involves insufficient insulin production; type 2 involves reduced cellular response to insulin.

questions.stem: Thermoregulation is an example of control by:
questions.explanation: Thermoregulation uses negative feedback to keep body temperature near the set point.

questions.stem: Which structure acts as the control centre for thermoregulation?
questions.explanation: Thermoregulation involves peripheral thermoreceptors, the hypothalamus and pituitary, thyroxin, and effectors such as muscle and adipose tissue.

questions.scenario: A person becomes too hot.
questions.stem: Which mechanism helps cool the body?
questions.explanation: When too hot, vasodilation and sweating increase heat loss; when cold, vasoconstriction and shivering conserve or generate heat.

questions.scenario: A person becomes too cold.
questions.stem: Which mechanism helps warm the body?
questions.explanation: When cold, shivering generates heat and vasoconstriction reduces heat loss; sweating and vasodilation cool the body.

questions.scenario: In cold conditions, blood vessels near the skin narrow.
questions.stem: This is called:
questions.explanation: Vasoconstriction narrows skin blood vessels to reduce heat loss when cold; vasodilation widens them to lose heat when hot.

questions.scenario: Birds and mammals regulate their body temperature.
questions.stem: They do so by:
questions.explanation: Birds and mammals regulate body temperature by both physiological and behavioural means.

questions.stem: Why is negative rather than positive feedback used in homeostasis?

options.text: It restores variables to the set point, keeping them stable
It amplifies changes away from the set point
It has no effect on variables

questions.explanation: Negative feedback stabilizes variables by returning them to the set point, which is why it is used in homeostasis.

questions.stem: Homeostatic variables are kept:

options.text: Within preset limits despite external changes
Exactly constant with no fluctuation ever

questions.explanation: Homeostasis keeps variables within preset limits despite fluctuations in the external environment.

questions.scenario: Insulin binds to target cells.
questions.stem: The effect is to:
questions.explanation: Insulin acts on target cells to lower blood glucose, part of the negative feedback control of glucose.

questions.stem: Which is a method of managing or preventing type 2 diabetes?

options.text: A healthy diet and physical activity

questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes risk is affected by lifestyle; diet and activity are important for prevention and treatment.

questions.scenario: Peripheral thermoreceptors detect skin temperature.
questions.stem: They send this information to the:
questions.explanation: Peripheral thermoreceptors send temperature information to the hypothalamus, the thermoregulatory control centre.

questions.scenario: Brown adipose tissue generates heat.
questions.stem: It does so by:
questions.explanation: Uncoupled respiration in brown adipose tissue generates heat, one of the human thermoregulation mechanisms.

questions.scenario: Hair erection occurs when a person is cold.
questions.stem: This helps by:

options.text: Trapping a layer of insulating air

questions.explanation: Hair erection traps an insulating layer of air, reducing heat loss when cold.

questions.stem: Which is a benefit to organisms of maintaining constant internal conditions?

options.text: Enzymes and cells can function reliably
It removes the need for enzymes

questions.explanation: Stable internal conditions allow enzymes and cells to function reliably despite external changes.

questions.scenario: Blood glucose regulation is an example of homeostasis by hormones.
questions.stem: The hormones are transported to target cells by the:
questions.explanation: Insulin and glucagon are transported in the blood to their target cells to regulate glucose.

questions.stem: Which tissues act as effectors in thermoregulation?
questions.explanation: Muscle and adipose tissue act as effectors of temperature change during thermoregulation.

questions.scenario: A variable falls below its set point.
questions.stem: Negative feedback will act to:

options.text: Raise it back toward the set point
Keep it below the set point

questions.explanation: Negative feedback returns variables to the set point from values both above and below it.

questions.stem: A key difference between type 1 and type 2 diabetes is:

options.text: Type 1 lacks insulin; type 2 involves reduced response to insulin
Type 1 involves reduced response; type 2 lacks insulin

questions.explanation: Type 1 diabetes involves insufficient insulin production; type 2 involves reduced cellular response to insulin.

questions.scenario: On a hot day, blood vessels near the skin widen.
questions.stem: This vasodilation:

options.text: Increases heat loss from the skin

questions.explanation: Vasodilation brings more blood near the skin surface, increasing heat loss when the body is too warm.

questions.stem: Which hormone is involved in thermoregulation?
questions.explanation: Thyroxin, along with the hypothalamus, pituitary and thermoreceptors, is involved in thermoregulation.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a homeostatic variable regulated in humans (per the syllabus)?
questions.explanation: Homeostatic variables include body temperature, blood pH, blood glucose and blood osmotic concentration; eye colour is not regulated.

questions.stem: Blood glucose regulation is an example of homeostasis achieved by:
questions.explanation: Blood glucose is regulated by the hormones insulin and glucagon acting through negative feedback.

questions.stem: A set point is:

options.text: The target value a variable is kept near

questions.explanation: Negative feedback returns a variable to its set point, the target value maintained by homeostasis.

questions.stem: Which pair correctly matches a response to being too hot?
questions.explanation: Sweating and vasodilation increase heat loss when hot; the others generate or conserve heat when cold.

questions.stem: Which organ has a key role in osmoregulation and excretion?
questions.explanation: The kidney plays a key role in osmoregulation (regulating osmotic concentration) and excretion.

questions.stem: What is the difference between excretion and osmoregulation?

options.text: Excretion removes wastes; osmoregulation controls osmotic concentration

questions.explanation: Excretion is removal of metabolic wastes; osmoregulation is regulation of osmotic concentration of body fluids.

questions.scenario: In the nephron, solutes are filtered from the blood plasma.
questions.stem: This filtration occurs at the:
questions.explanation: Ultrafiltration at the glomerulus and Bowman's capsule removes solutes from blood plasma into the filtrate.

questions.scenario: Useful substances such as glucose are recovered from the filtrate.
questions.stem: This reabsorption occurs mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Useful substances are reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule, leaving toxins and wastes in the filtrate.

questions.stem: The loop of Henle actively transports sodium ions in its ascending limb to:

options.text: Maintain high osmotic concentration in the medulla

questions.explanation: Active transport of sodium ions in the ascending limb maintains high medullary osmotic concentration, aiding water reabsorption.

questions.stem: The high osmotic concentration in the medulla facilitates:

options.text: Water reabsorption in the collecting ducts

questions.explanation: The high medullary osmotic concentration draws water out of the collecting ducts, allowing water reabsorption.

questions.scenario: The body needs to conserve water.
questions.stem: Which hormone increases water reabsorption in the collecting ducts?
questions.explanation: ADH from the pituitary increases water reabsorption in the collecting ducts by inserting aquaporins.

questions.scenario: ADH acts on the collecting duct cells.
questions.stem: It causes a change in the location of:

options.text: Aquaporins between vesicles and the cell membrane

questions.explanation: ADH switches the location of aquaporins between intracellular vesicles and the cell membrane, altering water reabsorption.

questions.scenario: Blood becomes too concentrated (high solute).
questions.stem: Which receptors detect this to trigger ADH release?
questions.explanation: Osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus detect blood osmotic concentration and adjust ADH secretion by the pituitary.

questions.scenario: During vigorous physical activity, blood supply changes.
questions.stem: Blood flow to skeletal muscles is likely to:
questions.explanation: During exercise, blood supply to skeletal muscles increases, while flow to organs such as the gut decreases.

questions.scenario: During vigorous physical activity, blood flow is redistributed.
questions.stem: Blood flow to the gut is likely to:
questions.explanation: During exercise, blood is redirected toward skeletal muscles, so flow to the gut decreases.

questions.stem: The units for osmotic concentration are:
questions.explanation: Osmotic concentration is measured in osmoles per litre (osmol L-1).

questions.scenario: After ultrafiltration, the filtrate still contains glucose and useful ions.
questions.stem: What happens to these useful substances?

options.text: They are reabsorbed into the blood

questions.explanation: Useful substances are reabsorbed (mainly in the proximal convoluted tubule), leaving wastes to be excreted in urine.

questions.stem: The loop of Henle is important because it:

options.text: Creates a concentration gradient in the medulla for water reabsorption

questions.explanation: By actively transporting sodium in the ascending limb, the loop of Henle builds the medullary gradient enabling water reabsorption.

questions.scenario: A person drinks a large amount of water, diluting the blood.
questions.stem: How does ADH secretion respond?

options.text: It decreases, so less water is reabsorbed
It increases, so more water is reabsorbed

questions.explanation: Dilute blood reduces ADH secretion, so fewer aquaporins are inserted and less water is reabsorbed, producing dilute urine.

questions.scenario: During sleep, blood supply patterns differ from during exercise.
questions.stem: This shows blood supply is:

options.text: Adjusted in response to changes in activity
The same in all organs at all times

questions.explanation: Blood supply to organs changes with activity, for example between sleep, vigorous exercise and wakeful rest.

questions.stem: Osmoregulation by the kidney helps maintain which homeostatic variable?
questions.explanation: Osmoregulation by the kidney maintains the blood osmotic concentration, one of the human homeostatic variables.
